Operating Systems: Design and Implementation

Chapter 1: Introduction

An operating system is a layer of software that manages the hardware resources of a computer and provides an abstraction layer for application programs. The operating system serves two primary functions: providing a clean, abstract set of resources for application programs (the top-down view), and managing the hardware resources among competing programs (the bottom-up view). Understanding operating systems requires knowledge of both the hardware they manage and the services they provide.

The history of operating systems closely follows the evolution of computer hardware. First-generation computers (1945-1955) had no operating systems — programmers interacted directly with the hardware using plug boards and later punch cards. Second-generation computers (1955-1965) introduced batch systems, where jobs were collected and processed sequentially. Third-generation computers (1965-1980) introduced multiprogramming, where the CPU switches between multiple jobs to keep it busy while waiting for I/O operations. Time-sharing systems extended this concept by giving each user a short time slice of the CPU, creating the illusion of having a dedicated machine.

Modern operating systems have evolved into complex systems supporting multiple users, multiple processes, networked operations, and sophisticated security mechanisms. The key concepts that underpin all operating systems include processes (the fundamental unit of execution), address spaces (the memory available to a process), files (abstractions for persistent storage), input/output (communication with external devices), protection (ensuring processes don't interfere with each other), and the shell (the user interface to the operating system).

Chapter 2: Processes and Threads

A process is fundamentally an instance of an executing program. Each process has its own address space, which typically contains the program text (code), data (global variables), and stack (local variables and return addresses). The operating system maintains a process table with entries for each process, containing information about the process's state, program counter, stack pointer, memory allocation, open files, and scheduling information.

Process creation occurs through system calls (fork in Unix, CreateProcess in Windows). In Unix, fork creates an exact copy of the parent process, and the child typically calls exec to replace its memory image with a new program. Process termination can be voluntary (normal exit or error exit) or involuntary (killed by another process or caused by a fatal error). Inter-process communication (IPC) mechanisms include pipes, message queues, shared memory, and semaphores.

Threads provide a way for a process to have multiple flows of execution. Each thread has its own program counter, registers, and stack, but shares the address space, open files, and other resources with other threads in the same process. Threads are lighter-weight than processes — creating and switching between threads is faster because there is no need to manage separate address spaces. The POSIX threads (pthreads) standard provides a portable API for thread creation and management.

Thread implementation can occur at the user level (managed by a user-space library without kernel knowledge), at the kernel level (managed directly by the operating system kernel), or as a hybrid approach. User-level threads are fast to create and switch but cannot take advantage of multiprocessor systems and suffer from the problem that if one thread blocks on I/O, all threads in the process are blocked. Kernel-level threads solve these problems but incur more overhead for creation and context switching.

Chapter 3: Memory Management

Memory management is one of the most critical functions of an operating system. The simplest approach is to have no abstraction at all — each program directly accesses physical memory. This approach, while simple, makes it impossible to run multiple programs simultaneously without them interfering with each other. Address spaces provide the necessary abstraction, giving each process its own view of memory.

Virtual memory is the technique that allows processes to use more memory than is physically available. The basic idea is to split the address space into fixed-size pages and the physical memory into frames of the same size. A page table maps virtual pages to physical frames. When a process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory (a page fault), the operating system loads it from disk. This mechanism allows the total virtual memory of all running processes to exceed the physical memory.

Page replacement algorithms determine which page to evict from physical memory when a page fault occurs and no free frames are available. The optimal algorithm (replacing the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future) is theoretically perfect but impossible to implement in practice because it requires knowledge of future memory references. Practical algorithms include FIFO (First-In, First-Out), LRU (Least Recently Used), and Clock (an approximation of LRU). The working set model identifies the set of pages a process is actively using and tries to keep this set in memory.

Segmentation provides a different memory abstraction where the address space is divided into variable-sized segments, each representing a logical unit such as a procedure, a data structure, or an array. Segmentation simplifies sharing and protection because each segment can have its own access permissions. In practice, most modern systems use a combination of segmentation and paging, where the virtual address space is first divided into segments and each segment is then divided into pages.

Chapter 4: File Systems

The file system is the most visible component of an operating system to users. Files provide a mechanism for long-term storage of data that survives the termination of the process that created it. The operating system abstracts the physical characteristics of the storage device, presenting a clean, device-independent interface for creating, reading, writing, and deleting files.

File system implementation involves several layers: the logical file system (managing metadata and directory structures), the file-organization module (translating logical block addresses to physical block addresses), and the basic file system (issuing I/O requests to the device drivers). Allocation methods determine how disk blocks are allocated to files: contiguous allocation places all blocks of a file in consecutive disk blocks, linked allocation connects blocks through pointers, and indexed allocation uses an index block to hold pointers to all blocks of a file. Modern file systems like ext4 and NTFS use sophisticated allocation strategies that combine elements of these basic approaches.

Directory structures organize files into a hierarchy. Early systems used flat (single-level) directories, but modern systems use tree-structured or acyclic-graph directories. In Unix-like systems, the directory tree starts from a root directory (/) and branches out to subdirectories. Hard links allow multiple directory entries to point to the same file, while symbolic links provide an indirect reference to a file through its path name. Mounting allows different file systems to be attached to the directory tree at specified mount points.

File system reliability is ensured through various mechanisms. Journaling file systems (like ext3, ext4, NTFS, and HFS+) maintain a log of intended changes before applying them, allowing the file system to be quickly recovered to a consistent state after a crash. Copy-on-write file systems (like ZFS and Btrfs) never overwrite existing data, instead writing modifications to new locations and updating pointers. Snapshots, checksums, and RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) provide additional layers of data protection.

Chapter 5: Input/Output

I/O management is a critical function of the operating system. I/O devices vary enormously in their characteristics: some are character devices (producing or consuming streams of bytes, like terminals and printers), while others are block devices (storing data in fixed-size blocks that can be independently addressed, like disks). The operating system must provide a uniform interface for application programs while accommodating the diverse characteristics of different devices.

Device drivers are the software modules that contain device-specific code. Each driver manages one type (or class) of device and presents a standard interface to the rest of the operating system. When an application performs an I/O operation, the request passes through several layers: the application calls a library function, which invokes a system call, which is dispatched to the appropriate device driver, which communicates with the device controller through hardware registers or memory-mapped I/O.

I/O scheduling determines the order in which I/O requests are serviced. For disk drives, scheduling algorithms include FCFS (First-Come, First-Served), SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First), SCAN (the elevator algorithm), C-SCAN (circular SCAN), and LOOK/C-LOOK (variants that reverse direction at the last request rather than at the end of the disk). The choice of algorithm depends on the workload characteristics and the trade-off between throughput and fairness.

Buffering and caching are essential techniques for improving I/O performance. A buffer is a memory area that stores data while it is being transferred between an I/O device and an application. Double buffering allows one buffer to be filled while another is being emptied, improving throughput. A cache stores copies of frequently accessed data in faster storage, reducing the number of accesses to slower devices. The operating system's buffer cache (or page cache in modern systems) caches disk blocks in memory, significantly reducing the number of disk I/O operations.

Chapter 6: Deadlocks

A deadlock occurs when a set of processes are blocked, each waiting for a resource held by another process in the set. Four conditions must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur: mutual exclusion (at least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode), hold and wait (a process must be holding at least one resource while waiting to acquire additional resources), no preemption (resources cannot be forcibly removed from processes), and circular wait (there must be a circular chain of processes, each waiting for a resource held by the next).

Strategies for dealing with deadlocks include prevention (ensuring that at least one of the four necessary conditions cannot hold), avoidance (making dynamic decisions about resource allocation to ensure the system never enters an unsafe state), detection (allowing deadlocks to occur and then detecting and resolving them), and ignorance (the ostrich algorithm — simply ignoring the problem under the assumption that deadlocks are rare). Most general-purpose operating systems, including Unix and Windows, take the last approach for most resources.

Deadlock avoidance uses the Banker's algorithm, which checks whether granting a resource request would leave the system in a safe state (a state where there exists a sequence in which all processes can complete). The algorithm requires advance knowledge of the maximum resource needs of each process, which is often impractical. Deadlock detection uses a resource allocation graph or its matrix-based equivalent to detect cycles (in the case of single-instance resources) or to determine whether a sequence of process completions exists that releases all resources.

Chapter 7: Virtualization

Virtualization technology allows multiple operating systems to run simultaneously on the same physical hardware. A hypervisor (or virtual machine monitor) creates and manages virtual machines, each of which appears to be a complete computer with its own hardware. Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisors run directly on the hardware, while Type 2 (hosted) hypervisors run on top of a conventional operating system.

Hardware support for virtualization has made modern hypervisors more efficient. Intel VT-x and AMD-V provide hardware extensions that allow hypervisors to run guest operating systems without modifying them. Memory virtualization is handled through nested page tables (also called second-level address translation), which translate guest virtual addresses to host physical addresses without requiring the hypervisor to maintain shadow page tables. I/O virtualization is provided by technologies like Intel VT-d, which allows direct device assignment to virtual machines.

Containers provide a lighter-weight alternative to full virtualization. Rather than virtualizing the entire hardware, containers share the host operating system's kernel and isolate applications through namespaces (which provide isolated views of system resources like process IDs, network interfaces, and file systems) and cgroups (which limit and account for resource usage). Docker popularized container technology, and Kubernetes provides orchestration for managing containers at scale. The trade-off between containers and virtual machines involves isolation strength (VMs provide stronger isolation) versus overhead (containers are more lightweight and start faster).
